% Each winner, price, and order is used exactly once (one-to-one matching).
% Ensure each winner is assigned exactly one price and one order.

% Ensure each price is assigned to exactly one winner and one order.
{auction(W, P, O) : winners(W), order(O)} = 1 :- price(P).

% Ensure each order is assigned to exactly one winner and one price.
{auction(W, P, O) : winners(W), price(P)} = 1 :- order(O).

% Clue 1: The butterfly auctioned 7th sold for 20 dollars less than the one won by Daniel.
% So: auction(_, P1, 7) and auction("Daniel", P2, _) with P1 = P2 - 20.
:- auction(_, P1, 7), auction("Daniel", P2, _), P1 != P2 - 20.

% Clue 2: The four butterflies are the one sold for $260, the one auctioned 4th,
% the one auctioned 7th, and the one won by Roland.
% These are all distinct items, so:
% - The item with price 260 is not the one with order 4, 7, or winner Roland.
% - The item with order 4 is not the one with price 260 or winner Roland.
% - The item with order 7 is not the one with price 260 or winner Roland.
% - The item won by Roland is not the one with price 260, order 4, or order 7.

:- auction(W, 260, 4).
:- auction(W, 260, 7).
:- auction(W, P, 4), W = "Roland".
:- auction(W, P, 7), W = "Roland".
:- auction("Roland", 260, O).
:- auction("Roland", P, 4).
:- auction("Roland", P, 7).

% Clue 3: The butterfly won by Gabriel was the 8th lot.
:- auction("Gabriel", P, O), O != 8.
